Client 2: And I’m Jennifer—just, like, clarity. I want to know what happens if the market drops right before we stop working.
Advisor: Totally fair. I’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll put some guardrails around the risk. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your household gross income per year right now?
Client 1: Household gross is 104800 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that income, is it mostly from work, pension, a mix?
Client 1: It’s pension income, annual. Between us it totals 104800 annually.

Client 1: And I’m Client 1, self-employed, yeah.
Advisor: Perfect. Next, on spending—about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in? Mortgage or rent, utilities, food, travel, the whole thing.
Client 2: Roughly four thousand a month—like 4000 total monthly expenses.
Advisor: Okay, 4000 a month. Any regular debt payments on top of that—credit cards, car loans, student loans—anything like that?
Client 1: No. Monthly debt payments total is 0.

Client 2: And I’m Jennifer—just, like, clarity. I want to know what happens if the market drops right before we stop working.
Advisor: Totally fair. I’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll put some guardrails around the risk. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your household gross income per year right now?
Client 1: Household gross is 104800 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that income, is it mostly from work, pension, a mix?
Client 1: It’s pension income, annual. Between us it totals 104800 annually.

Client 2: Um… I’d guess like six hundred-something? I don’t know the exact.
Advisor: That’s fine. Rober, do you have the total handy—ballpark is okay, but if you know the exact, even better.
Client 1: Yeah, I have it. Investable assets total is 692300.
Advisor: Thanks. We’ll break that into the actual accounts in a minute. Before we do, do you have any other debts at all—like a mortgage on the house?
Client 2: No mortgage. The house is paid off.

Client 2: Yes, I have a private markets investment—48000—through an insurance company.
Advisor: Thanks. And then the home—just to capture it for the net worth picture—what do you estimate the primary residence value at?
Client 2: The primary residence is 305000, also through an insurance company.
Advisor: Okay. And no debt against it, as you said. Last bucket I want to make sure we cover today is protection—life insurance. Rober, do you have a policy in place?
Client 1: Yes. It’s life insurance on me—monthly premium is 100, coverage amount is 565100, and it’s covered until 2043-09-02.

Client 2: And I’m Jennifer—just, like, clarity. I want to know what happens if the market drops right before we stop working.
Advisor: Totally fair. I’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll put some guardrails around the risk. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your household gross income per year right now?
Client 1: Household gross is 104800 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that income, is it mostly from work, pension, a mix?
Client 1: It’s pension income, annual. Between us it totals 104800 annually.

Client 2: And I’m Jennifer—just, like, clarity. I want to know what happens if the market drops right before we stop working.
Advisor: Totally fair. I’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll put some guardrails around the risk. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your household gross income per year right now?

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that income, is it mostly from work, pension, a mix?
Client 1: It’s pension income, annual. Between us it totals 104800 annually.

Advisor: Totally fair. I’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll put some guardrails around the risk. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your household gross income per year right now?
Client 1: Household gross is 104800 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that income, is it mostly from work, pension, a mix?
Client 1: It’s pension income, annual. Between us it totals 104800 annually.

Advisor: Hi Rober, hi Jennifer—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Rober. Win is: can we retire in a few years without doing something dumb tax-wise, and still stay pretty growth-oriented.
Client 2: And I’m Jennifer—just, like, clarity. I want to know what happens if the market drops right before we stop working.
Advisor: Totally fair. I’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll put some guardrails around the risk. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your household gross income per year right now?
Client 1: Household gross is 104800 a year.

Advisor: Got it. And country-wise, you’re in the US, right?
Client 1: Yes, US.
Advisor: Perfect. Next—just so I’m clear on how we’re working together—are you engaging me through an RIA, meaning a registered investment advisor relationship?
Client 1: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great. Now, risk—when we talked about staying growth-oriented as retirement gets closer, how would you label your overall risk tolerance?

Advisor: Hi Rober, hi Jennifer—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Rober. Win is: can we retire in a few years without doing something dumb tax-wise, and still stay pretty growth-oriented.
Client 2: And I’m Jennifer—just, like, clarity. I want to know what happens if the market drops right before we stop working.
Advisor: Totally fair. I’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll put some guardrails around the risk. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your household gross income per year right now?
Client 1: Household gross is 104800 a year.

Advisor: Okay. And no debt against it, as you said. Last bucket I want to make sure we cover today is protection—life insurance. Rober, do you have a policy in place?
Client 1: Yes. It’s life insurance on me—monthly premium is 100, coverage amount is 565100, and it’s covered until 2043-09-02.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. So we’ve got income, spending, no debt payments, assets, and the life policy captured. Next time we’ll dig into goals and how aggressive you want to stay as retirement gets closer.
Advisor: Before we wrap, I want to tighten up the debt and property piece so the net worth picture is clean. Jennifer, you mentioned the house is paid off—what value should I use for the home?
Client 2: Use 305000 for the primary residence value.

Client 1: Yes, exactly.
Advisor: Perfect. That’s all I needed for today. Next meeting we’ll map goals and timelines and stress-test what “aggressive” looks like with guardrails.
Advisor: Actually—before we hop off, I realized I didn’t ask a couple household basics that matter for planning. Rober, Jennifer, are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And dependents—anyone financially dependent on you right now? Kids still on the payroll, or anyone you support?

Client 1: Yes, number of adults is 2.
Advisor: Perfect. Last one in this bucket: when you think about taxes, what bracket do you usually assume for your marginal rate—ballpark is fine.
Client 2: We usually assume 35%.
Advisor: Great—thanks, Jennifer. That helps me frame the after-tax side when we stress-test the retirement timing.
Client 1: Yeah, especially with the pension—taxes are the part I don’t want to hand-wave.

Client 2: And I’m Jennifer—just, like, clarity. I want to know what happens if the market drops right before we stop working.

Client 2: Right, I’m inactive right now—no job income coming in.
